text: Alright, so here we go. Today I'm presenting a robotic low anterior resection with TME. This patient was an early fifties female. She was presented with a locally advanced mid rectal adenocarcinoma. As you can see from how juicy it is already, she has undergone neoadjuvant therapy. She developed C. diff colitis just after completing that neoadjuvant therapy, and it was actually recurrent after that. And so she had a bit of a delay. This was, I believe, about ten weeks after the completion of her neoadjuvant.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, approaching the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The dissection reveals the inferior mesenteric vein and the unexpected low takeoff of the left colic branch. The surgeon adjusts the dissection to separate tissues between the IMA and the vein, aiming to locate the IMA more accurately.

The surgeon finalizes the dissection of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), noting the parallel course of the patient's left colic artery alongside the IMV. A vessel sealer is used to cauterize and divide the IMV, with initial difficulties due to the instrument's stickiness, requiring a change to a new vessel sealer. The dissection progresses medially to laterally towards the splenic flexure, without entering the lesser sac, and transitions to a lateral to medial approach to connect dissection planes.

The surgeon completes the final mobilization of the sigmoid colon, focusing on the left-sided dissection down to the level where the medial to lateral dissection began on the right side. The uterus is suspended using a Keith needle, preparing for the start of the total mesorectal excision (TME). The port positions and instrumentation include two left arms, with the tip up in the number one position, and a smoke evacuator is used for suction and irrigation.

The surgeon uses scissors to dissect as low as possible on the right side, encountering the right iliac vessels. Instruments are repositioned to the patient's left, with the tip elevated to number four for retraction of the rectum, uterus, and vagina. The posterior dissection is taken down as low as possible before addressing anterior attachments. The tumor is located just below the peritoneal reflection in the pouch of Douglas, with visible scarring indicating an excellent response to neoadjuvant therapy.

The surgeon identifies the upper part of the tumor, which is constricted and scarred. The posterior dissection is performed first, followed by the anterior dissection where significant scarring is present. A thin septum between the vagina and rectum is visible, with a large rectocele posing a challenge. The surgeon carefully dissects to separate the vagina from the rectum without compromising the oncologic quality, while avoiding entry into the vagina.

The surgeon navigates a dissection plane anteriorly, working around the rectum from posterior to anterior and to the sides. A rolled-up Raytec sponge is used for retracting the mesorectum, minimizing the risk of violating it during retraction. The surgeon cuts the sponge in half for better handling. In post-radiation tissue, cautery use generates smoke, which is managed with a suction irrigator in the left hand. A 30-degree scope is used, flipped to improve visualization deep in the pelvis as the surgeon mobilizes the mesentery anteriorly.

The surgeon completes the anterior dissection, carefully peeling tissue to avoid catching the back of the vagina due to the presence of a rectocele. A stapler is prepared, and two blue loads are used, with a 45mm stapler chosen for its suitability in the deep pelvis. The wide female pelvis facilitates the procedure.

The surgeon defines the mesenteric pedicle and progresses towards the proximal resection margin, clearing the area. An abbreviated Pfannenstiel incision is made, approximately four centimeters in length, to extract the specimen. The anvil is placed externally and then reinserted. The procedure continues laparoscopically with the assistance of a skilled assistant, ensuring the anvil reaches the desired location without tension.

The surgeon uses a 33mm EEA stapler to clean out the remaining rectum, ensuring the colon is not twisted and the mesentery is correctly positioned before firing the stapler. The ends are brought together smoothly.

The surgeon performs a rigid proctoscopy and conducts a leak test. Images of the resected specimen are shown, confirming no residual cancer, with fourteen negative lymph nodes and an intact mesentery. The distal margin measures 4.2 centimeters.
